question:
4. Let \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \) be a sequence of positive real numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sup n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right) \geq 1
\]

Show that the number 1 on the right-hand side of this inequality cannot be replaced by any larger number. (The symbol lim sup is sometimes written lim.)
solution:
Solution. Suppose that for some fixed integer \( k \),
\[
n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right) \leq 1
\]
for all \( n \geq k \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+a_{n+1} & \leq \frac{n+1}{n} a_{n}, \\
\frac{a_{n}}{n} & \geq \frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{a_{n+1}}{n+1}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( n \geq k \). Accordingly we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{a_{k}}{k} & \geq \frac{1}{k+1}+\frac{a_{k+1}}{k+1} \geq \frac{1}{k+1}+\frac{1}{k+2}+\frac{a_{k+2}}{k+2} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{k+1}+\frac{1}{k+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{k+p}+\frac{a_{k+p}}{k+p} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{k+1}+\frac{1}{k+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{k+p}
\end{aligned}
\]
for each \( p \). However, this is impossible since the harmonic series diverges.
Thus for any \( k \) there exists an \( n \geq k \) such that
\[
n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right)>1 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{n \rightarrow \infty} n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right) \geq 1 .
\]

If we take \( a_{n}=n \log n \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right) & =\frac{1+(n+1) \log (n+1)-n \log n}{\log n} \\
& =\frac{1}{\log n}\left[1+n \log \frac{n+1}{n}+\log (n+1)\right] \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\log n}[2+\log (n+1)]
\end{aligned}
\]
since \( \log (1+x) \leq x \) for all \( x>-1 \). Since the right side has limit 1 , we have, for the particular sequence,
\[
\limsup _{n \rightarrow \infty} n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right) \leq 1 .
\]

Thus we cannot increase the bound 1.
We could also take \( a_{n}=n^{1+\epsilon} \) where \( \epsilon>0 \) and obtain
\[
\limsup _{n-\infty} n\left(\frac{1+a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}-1\right)=1+\epsilon .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
4. Let \( \{seqterm\} \) be a sequence of positive real numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \sup indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right) \geq 1
\]

Show that the number 1 on the right-hand side of this inequality cannot be replaced by any larger number. (The symbol lim sup is sometimes written lim.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose that for some fixed integer \( startidx \),
\[
indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right) \leq 1
\]
for all \( indexvar \geq startidx \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+seqnext & \leq \frac{indexvar+1}{indexvar} \, seqterm, \\
\frac{seqterm}{indexvar} & \geq \frac{1}{indexvar+1}+\frac{seqnext}{indexvar+1}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( indexvar \geq startidx \). Accordingly we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{seqtermk}{startidx} & \geq \frac{1}{startidx+1}+\frac{seqnextk}{startidx+1} \geq \frac{1}{startidx+1}+\frac{1}{startidx+2}+\frac{seqnextk2}{startidx+2} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{startidx+1}+\frac{1}{startidx+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{startidx+iteratep}+\frac{seqkplusp}{startidx+iteratep} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{startidx+1}+\frac{1}{startidx+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{startidx+iteratep}
\end{aligned}
\]
for each \( iteratep \). However, this is impossible since the harmonic series diverges.
Thus for any \( startidx \) there exists an \( indexvar \geq startidx \) such that
\[
indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right)>1 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right) \geq 1 .
\]

If we take \( seqterm=indexvar \log indexvar \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right) & =\frac{1+(indexvar+1) \log (indexvar+1)-indexvar \log indexvar}{\log indexvar} \\
& =\frac{1}{\log indexvar}\left[1+indexvar \log \frac{indexvar+1}{indexvar}+\log (indexvar+1)\right] \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\log indexvar}[2+\log (indexvar+1)]
\end{aligned}
\]
since \( \log (1+varxplaceholder) \leq varxplaceholder \) for all \( varxplaceholder>-1 \). Since the right side has limit 1, we have, for the particular sequence,
\[
\limsup _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right) \leq 1 .
\]

Thus we cannot increase the bound 1.
We could also take \( seqterm=indexvar^{1+smalltol} \) where \( smalltol>0 \) and obtain
\[
\limsup _{indexvar-\infty} indexvar\left(\frac{1+seqnext}{seqterm}-1\right)=1+smalltol .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. Let \( \left\{driftwood\right\} \) be a sequence of positive real numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{jackalope \rightarrow \infty} \sup jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right) \geq 1
\]

Show that the number 1 on the right-hand side of this inequality cannot be replaced by any larger number. (The symbol lim sup is sometimes written lim.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose that for some fixed integer \( tumbleweed \),
\[
jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right) \leq 1
\]
for all \( jackalope \geq tumbleweed \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+moonlight & \leq \frac{jackalope+1}{jackalope} driftwood, \\
\frac{driftwood}{jackalope} & \geq \frac{1}{jackalope+1}+\frac{moonlight}{jackalope+1}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( jackalope \geq tumbleweed \). Accordingly we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{sandcastle}{tumbleweed} & \geq \frac{1}{tumbleweed+1}+\frac{peppermint}{tumbleweed+1} \geq \frac{1}{tumbleweed+1}+\frac{1}{tumbleweed+2}+\frac{horseshoe}{tumbleweed+2} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{tumbleweed+1}+\frac{1}{tumbleweed+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{tumbleweed+goldfish}+\frac{raincloud}{tumbleweed+goldfish} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{tumbleweed+1}+\frac{1}{tumbleweed+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{tumbleweed+goldfish}
\end{aligned}
\]
for each \( goldfish \). However, this is impossible since the harmonic series diverges.
Thus for any \( tumbleweed \) there exists an \( jackalope \geq tumbleweed \) such that
\[
jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right)>1 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{jackalope \rightarrow \infty} jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right) \geq 1 .
\]

If we take \( driftwood=jackalope \log jackalope \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right) & =\frac{1+(jackalope+1) \log (jackalope+1)-jackalope \log jackalope}{\log jackalope} \\
& =\frac{1}{\log jackalope}\left[1+jackalope \log \frac{jackalope+1}{jackalope}+\log (jackalope+1)\right] \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\log jackalope}[2+\log (jackalope+1)]
\end{aligned}
\]
since \( \log (1+jellybean) \leq jellybean \) for all \( jellybean>-1 \). Since the right side has limit 1 , we have, for the particular sequence,
\[
\limsup _{jackalope \rightarrow \infty} jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right) \leq 1 .
\]

Thus we cannot increase the bound 1.
We could also take \( driftwood=jackalope^{1+buttercup} \) where \( buttercup>0 \) and obtain
\[
\limsup _{jackalope-\infty} jackalope\left(\frac{1+moonlight}{driftwood}-1\right)=1+buttercup .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let \( \left\{fixedvalue\right\} \) be a sequence of positive real numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{continuumindex \rightarrow \infty} \sup continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right) \geq 1
\]

Show that the number 1 on the right-hand side of this inequality cannot be replaced by any larger number.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose that for some fixed integer \( variableindex \),
\[
continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right) \leq 1
\]
for all \( continuumindex \geq variableindex \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+previousvalue & \leq \frac{continuumindex+1}{continuumindex} fixedvalue, \\
\frac{fixedvalue}{continuumindex} & \geq \frac{1}{continuumindex+1}+\frac{previousvalue}{continuumindex+1}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( continuumindex \geq variableindex \). Accordingly we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{stabledata}{variableindex} & \geq \frac{1}{variableindex+1}+\frac{stagnantdatum}{variableindex+1} \geq \frac{1}{variableindex+1}+\frac{1}{variableindex+2}+\frac{motionlessinfo}{variableindex+2} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{variableindex+1}+\frac{1}{variableindex+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{variableindex+stableoffset}+\frac{unchangingrecord}{variableindex+stableoffset} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{variableindex+1}+\frac{1}{variableindex+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{variableindex+stableoffset}
\end{aligned}
\]
for each \( stableoffset \). However, this is impossible since the harmonic series diverges.
Thus for any \( variableindex \) there exists an \( continuumindex \geq variableindex \) such that
\[
continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right)>1 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{continuumindex \rightarrow \infty} continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right) \geq 1 .
\]

If we take \( fixedvalue=continuumindex \log continuumindex \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right) & =\frac{1+(continuumindex+1) \log (continuumindex+1)-continuumindex \log continuumindex}{\log continuumindex} \\
& =\frac{1}{\log continuumindex}\left[1+continuumindex \log \frac{continuumindex+1}{continuumindex}+\log (continuumindex+1)\right] \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\log continuumindex}[2+\log (continuumindex+1)]
\end{aligned}
\]
since \( \log (1+constantinput) \leq constantinput \) for all \( constantinput>-1 \). Since the right side has limit 1 , we have, for the particular sequence,
\[
\limsup _{continuumindex \rightarrow \infty} continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right) \leq 1 .
\]

Thus we cannot increase the bound 1.
We could also take \( fixedvalue=continuumindex^{1+largenumber} \) where \( largenumber>0 \) and obtain
\[
\limsup _{continuumindex-\infty} continuumindex\left(\frac{1+previousvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right)=1+largenumber .
\]

garbled_string.question:
4. Let \( \left\{vxcnsdpe\right\} \) be a sequence of positive real numbers. Show that
\[
\lim _{zlmqrtyh \rightarrow \infty} \sup zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right) \geq 1
\]

Show that the number 1 on the right-hand side of this inequality cannot be replaced by any larger number. (The symbol lim sup is sometimes written lim.)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose that for some fixed integer \( psnhxdaw \),
\[
zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right) \leq 1
\]
for all \( zlmqrtyh \geq psnhxdaw \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+kljhrwtu & \leq \frac{zlmqrtyh+1}{zlmqrtyh} vxcnsdpe, \\
\frac{vxcnsdpe}{zlmqrtyh} & \geq \frac{1}{zlmqrtyh+1}+\frac{kljhrwtu}{zlmqrtyh+1}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( zlmqrtyh \geq psnhxdaw \). Accordingly we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{mbqrzdyo}{psnhxdaw} & \geq \frac{1}{psnhxdaw+1}+\frac{wnfzpxis}{psnhxdaw+1} \geq \frac{1}{psnhxdaw+1}+\frac{1}{psnhxdaw+2}+\frac{ydrgvkqe}{psnhxdaw+2} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{psnhxdaw+1}+\frac{1}{psnhxdaw+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{psnhxdaw+tlkquorz}+\frac{gqsldvma}{psnhxdaw+tlkquorz} \\
& \geq \frac{1}{psnhxdaw+1}+\frac{1}{psnhxdaw+2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{psnhxdaw+tlkquorz}
\end{aligned}
\]
for each \( tlkquorz \). However, this is impossible since the harmonic series diverges.
Thus for any \( psnhxdaw \) there exists an \( zlmqrtyh \geq psnhxdaw \) such that
\[
zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right)>1 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{zlmqrtyh \rightarrow \infty} zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right) \geq 1 .
\]

If we take \( vxcnsdpe=zlmqrtyh \log zlmqrtyh \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right) & =\frac{1+(zlmqrtyh+1) \log (zlmqrtyh+1)-zlmqrtyh \log zlmqrtyh}{\log zlmqrtyh} \\
& =\frac{1}{\log zlmqrtyh}\left[1+zlmqrtyh \log \frac{zlmqrtyh+1}{zlmqrtyh}+\log (zlmqrtyh+1)\right] \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\log zlmqrtyh}[2+\log (zlmqrtyh+1)]
\end{aligned}
\]
since \( \log (1+rnfvqzie) \leq rnfvqzie \) for all \( rnfvqzie>-1 \). Since the right side has limit 1, we have, for the particular sequence,
\[
\limsup _{zlmqrtyh \rightarrow \infty} zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right) \leq 1 .
\]

Thus we cannot increase the bound 1.
We could also take \( vxcnsdpe=zlmqrtyh^{1+cjpwexhm} \) where \( cjpwexhm>0 \) and obtain
\[
\limsup _{zlmqrtyh-\infty} zlmqrtyh\left(\frac{1+kljhrwtu}{vxcnsdpe}-1\right)=1+cjpwexhm .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 1$ be a fixed positive integer and let $\,(a_{n})_{n\ge 1}$ be an arbitrary sequence of positive real numbers.  
For every $n\ge 1$ put  

\[
E_{n}\;:=\;n\Bigl(\tfrac{m+a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}-1\Bigr).\tag{$\star$}
\]

1. (Universal lower bound)  Prove that  

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty} E_{n}\;\ge\;m. \tag{1}
\]

2. (Sharpness of the constant)  Show that the number $m$ in (1) is best possible: for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a positive sequence $(a_{n})$ such that  

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty} E_{n}\;\le\;m+\varepsilon .
\]

(In particular one can have $\limsup_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m$.)

3. (Asymptotic structure of the extremal sequences)  
Write $L_{n}:=a_{n}/n$ $(n\ge 1)$.  Prove that  

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m \tag{2}
\]

holds if and only if  

\[
\text{(a)}\;L_{n}\longrightarrow\infty ,
\qquad
\text{(b)}\;(n+m)\bigl(L_{n+m}/L_{n}-1\bigr)\longrightarrow 0. \tag{3}
\]

Hence the sequences for which (2) is true are precisely those of the form  

\[
a_{n}=nL_{n}\quad\text{with}\quad L_{n}\longrightarrow\infty
\;\text{ and }\;L_{n+m}/L_{n}\longrightarrow 1 .
\]

Give a few explicit examples (for instance $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha>0$, or  
$a_{n}=n\exp[(\log\log n)^{\beta}]$ with $0<\beta<1$) to illustrate the variety of such extremal sequences.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we set  

\[
b_{n}:=\frac{a_{n}}{n}\qquad(n\ge 1),
\quad\text{so that}\quad a_{n}=nb_{n}>0 .
\]

A direct substitution in $(\star)$ gives the key identity  

\[
E_{n}=m+\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\qquad(n\ge 1). \tag{4}
\]

  
Part 1.  Proof of the universal bound (1)  
  

Assume, on the contrary, that $\limsup_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m-2\delta$ for some $\delta>0$.  
Then there is $N$ such that  

\[
E_{n}\le m-\delta\qquad(n\ge N). \tag{5}
\]

From (4) we obtain, for $n\ge N$,  

\[
\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\le-\delta. \tag{6}
\]

Because the first summand in (6) is non-negative, the second one is negative, hence $b_{n+m}<b_{n}$.  
Rearranging (6) gives  

\[
b_{n}-b_{n+m}\;\ge\;\frac{m}{n+m}. \tag{7}
\]

Let $n_{k}:=N+km$ $(k\ge 0)$.  
Summing (7) for $k=0,1,\dots,p-1$ yields  

\[
b_{n_{p}}\;\le\;b_{N}-m\sum_{k=1}^{p}\frac{1}{n_{k}}\;.
\]

The harmonic series diverges, so the right-hand side eventually becomes negative, contradicting $b_{n}>0$.  
Hence (5) is impossible and (1) is established. \blacksquare 



  
Part 2.  Sharpness of the constant  
  

(a) Example with $\displaystyle\limsup E_{n}=m$.  
Let $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha>0$ and $n\ge 3$.  
By the Taylor expansion $\log(n+m)=\log n+\dfrac{m}{n}+O(1/n^{2})$ we get  

\[
\frac{a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}
      =1+\frac{m}{n}+\frac{\alpha m}{n\log n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right),
\qquad
\frac{m}{a_{n}}=O\!\left(\frac{1}{n(\log n)^{\alpha}}\right).
\]

Substituting in (4) gives  

\[
E_{n}=m+\frac{m}{(\log n)^{\alpha}}+\frac{\alpha m}{\log n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)=m+o(1),
\]
hence $\limsup E_{n}=m$.

(b) Example with $\displaystyle\limsup E_{n}=m+\varepsilon$.  
Fix $\varepsilon>0$ and take $a_{n}=n^{1+\varepsilon/m}$.  Then  

\[
\frac{a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}
      =(1+\tfrac{m}{n})^{1+\varepsilon/m}
      =1+\Bigl(1+\tfrac{\varepsilon}{m}\Bigr)\frac{m}{n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right),
\qquad
\frac{m}{a_{n}}=O\!\bigl(n^{-1-\varepsilon/m}\bigr),
\]
and (4) yields  

\[
E_{n}=m+\varepsilon+O\!\bigl(n^{-\varepsilon/m}\bigr)\longrightarrow m+\varepsilon .
\]

Because $\varepsilon>0$ is arbitrary, the constant $m$ in (1) cannot be increased. \blacksquare 



  
Part 3.  Asymptotic description of the extremals  
  

We prove that (2) is equivalent to the pair of conditions (3).

Necessity.  
Assume $E_{n}\to m$.  From (4) we have  

\[
E_{n}-m=\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0. \tag{8}
\]

Step 1.  Show that $b_{n}\to\infty$ (i.e. (3a)).  
Suppose, to the contrary, that $b_{n}$ is not unbounded.  
Then there exist $B>0$ and an infinite subsequence $(n_{k})$ with $b_{n_{k}}\le B$.  
Let  

\[
\varepsilon:=\frac{m}{2B}>0 .
\]

Because $E_{n}\to m$, we can choose this subsequence so that  

\[
\lvert E_{n_{k}}-m\rvert<\varepsilon \qquad\text{for all }k. \tag{9}
\]

Applying (8) to these indices and using $b_{n_{k}}\le B$ gives  

\[
(n_{k}+m)\Bigl(1-\tfrac{b_{n_{k}+m}}{b_{n_{k}}}\Bigr)
      =\frac{m}{b_{n_{k}}}-(E_{n_{k}}-m)
      \ge\frac{m}{B}-\varepsilon
      =\varepsilon . \tag{10}
\]

Hence  

\[
b_{n_{k}+m}\;\le\;b_{n_{k}}\Bigl(1-\frac{\varepsilon}{n_{k}+m}\Bigr). \tag{11}
\]

Iterating (11) over the arithmetic progression $n_{k,r}:=n_{k}+rm$ $(r\ge 0)$ gives  

\[
b_{n_{k,r}}\;\le\;B
\exp\!\Bigl(-\varepsilon\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}\frac{1}{n_{k}+jm+m}\Bigr)
\longrightarrow 0\quad(r\to\infty). \tag{12}
\]

Choose $r$ so large that  

\[
b_{n_{k,r}}\;<\;\frac{m}{2(n_{k,r}+m)}. \tag{13}
\]

Returning to (4) with $n=n_{k,r}$ and using $\dfrac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}>0$ we obtain  

\[
E_{n_{k,r}}-m
      \;\ge\;\frac{m}{b_{n_{k,r}}}-(n_{k,r}+m)
      \;>\;2(n_{k,r}+m)-(n_{k,r}+m)
      \;=\;n_{k,r}+m .
\]

Thus $E_{n_{k,r}}-m$ is arbitrarily large, contradicting $E_{n}\to m$.  
Therefore the assumption was false and $b_{n}\to\infty$.  This proves (3a).

Step 2.  Deduce (3b).  
Since $b_{n}\to\infty$, the first term on the right-hand side of (8) tends to $0$; hence  

\[
(n+m)\Bigl(\tfrac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\longrightarrow 0 ,
\]
which is exactly (3b).  Necessity is established.

Sufficiency.  
Conversely, if (3a)-(3b) hold, then $\dfrac{m}{b_{n}}\to 0$ and the last term in (4) also tends to $0$, so $E_{n}\to m$. \blacksquare 



  
Examples of extremal sequences  
  

1. $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$, $\alpha>0$.  
   Then $L_{n}=(\log n)^{\alpha}\to\infty$, and a direct calculation gives  
\[
(n+m)\bigl(L_{n+m}/L_{n}-1\bigr)
      =\frac{\alpha m}{\log n}+O\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{(\log n)^{2}}\bigr)\longrightarrow 0.
\]

2. $a_{n}=n\exp\!\bigl[(\log\log n)^{\beta}\bigr]$, $0<\beta<1$.  
   Here $\log L_{n}=(\log\log n)^{\beta}$, and  
\[
\log\!\frac{L_{n+m}}{L_{n}}
      =(\log\log(n+m))^{\beta}-(\log\log n)^{\beta}
      =O\!\Bigl(\frac{1}{n(\log n)^{1-\beta}}\Bigr),
\]
so $(3)$ is satisfied.

3. More generally, any $a_{n}=nL_{n}$ with $L_{n}$ slowly varying (with step $m$) and $L_{n}\to\infty$ fulfils $E_{n}\to m$, and every sequence with this property is of that form. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-order interaction:   
  The expression now links the term aₙ to a_{n+m} across an arbitrary positive
  offset m.  The proof must control the evolution of the sequence in jumps of
  length m, rather than in consecutive terms.

• Divergent series with stride m:  
  Deriving the contradiction requires building an m-step harmonic series
  ∑_{j}1/(k+jm), not the ordinary harmonic series that sufficed in the
  original problem.  Handling this necessitates a careful index-shifting
  argument and a non-trivial comparison with the usual ∑1/ℓ.

• Parameter dependence:  
  Both the inequality and the optimal constant depend explicitly on m.  One
  must show simultaneously that  
  (a) the lower bound scales exactly with m, and  
  (b) the bound is tight for every m, by constructing appropriate families of
  test sequences.  This introduces an additional layer of quantification and
  verification absent from the original kernel.

• More intricate recursion:  
  Instead of a single forward estimate, the proof demands an m-step recursive
  inequality and its iteration, leading to a telescoping sum with variable
  denominators.  Careful bookkeeping of these denominators is essential to
  guarantee divergence.

Overall, the enhanced variant forces competitors to generalise the harmonic-series
argument, manage non-unit step sizes, and track a parameter that permeates every
stage of the reasoning, making the problem substantially more technical and
conceptually demanding than the original.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 1$ be a fixed positive integer and let $\,(a_{n})_{n\ge 1}$ be an arbitrary sequence of positive real numbers.  
For every $n\ge 1$ put  

\[
E_{n}\;:=\;n\Bigl(\tfrac{m+a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}-1\Bigr).\tag{$\star$}
\]

1. (Universal lower bound)  Prove that  

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty} E_{n}\;\ge\;m. \tag{1}
\]

2. (Sharpness of the constant)  Show that the number $m$ in (1) is best possible: for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a positive sequence $(a_{n})$ such that  

\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty} E_{n}\;\le\;m+\varepsilon .
\]

(In particular one can have $\limsup_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m$.)

3. (Asymptotic structure of the extremal sequences)  
Write $L_{n}:=a_{n}/n$ $(n\ge 1)$.  Prove that  

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m \tag{2}
\]

holds if and only if  

\[
\text{(a)}\;L_{n}\longrightarrow\infty ,
\qquad
\text{(b)}\;(n+m)\bigl(L_{n+m}/L_{n}-1\bigr)\longrightarrow 0. \tag{3}
\]

Hence the sequences for which (2) is true are precisely those of the form  

\[
a_{n}=nL_{n}\quad\text{with}\quad L_{n}\longrightarrow\infty
\;\text{ and }\;L_{n+m}/L_{n}\longrightarrow 1 .
\]

Give a few explicit examples (for instance $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha>0$, or  
$a_{n}=n\exp[(\log\log n)^{\beta}]$ with $0<\beta<1$) to illustrate the variety of such extremal sequences.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we set  

\[
b_{n}:=\frac{a_{n}}{n}\qquad(n\ge 1),
\quad\text{so that}\quad a_{n}=nb_{n}>0 .
\]

A direct substitution in $(\star)$ gives the key identity  

\[
E_{n}=m+\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\qquad(n\ge 1). \tag{4}
\]

  
Part 1.  Proof of the universal bound (1)  
  

Assume, on the contrary, that $\limsup_{n\to\infty}E_{n}=m-2\delta$ for some $\delta>0$.  
Then there is $N$ such that  

\[
E_{n}\le m-\delta\qquad(n\ge N). \tag{5}
\]

From (4) we obtain, for $n\ge N$,  

\[
\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\le-\delta. \tag{6}
\]

Because the first summand in (6) is non-negative, the second one is negative, hence $b_{n+m}<b_{n}$.  
Rearranging (6) gives  

\[
b_{n}-b_{n+m}\;\ge\;\frac{m}{n+m}. \tag{7}
\]

Let $n_{k}:=N+km$ $(k\ge 0)$.  
Summing (7) for $k=0,1,\dots,p-1$ yields  

\[
b_{n_{p}}\;\le\;b_{N}-m\sum_{k=1}^{p}\frac{1}{n_{k}}\;.
\]

The harmonic series diverges, so the right-hand side eventually becomes negative, contradicting $b_{n}>0$.  
Hence (5) is impossible and (1) is established. \blacksquare 



  
Part 2.  Sharpness of the constant  
  

(a) Example with $\displaystyle\limsup E_{n}=m$.  
Let $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha>0$ and $n\ge 3$.  
By the Taylor expansion $\log(n+m)=\log n+\dfrac{m}{n}+O(1/n^{2})$ we get  

\[
\frac{a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}
      =1+\frac{m}{n}+\frac{\alpha m}{n\log n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right),
\qquad
\frac{m}{a_{n}}=O\!\left(\frac{1}{n(\log n)^{\alpha}}\right).
\]

Substituting in (4) gives  

\[
E_{n}=m+\frac{m}{(\log n)^{\alpha}}+\frac{\alpha m}{\log n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)=m+o(1),
\]
hence $\limsup E_{n}=m$.

(b) Example with $\displaystyle\limsup E_{n}=m+\varepsilon$.  
Fix $\varepsilon>0$ and take $a_{n}=n^{1+\varepsilon/m}$.  Then  

\[
\frac{a_{n+m}}{a_{n}}
      =(1+\tfrac{m}{n})^{1+\varepsilon/m}
      =1+\Bigl(1+\tfrac{\varepsilon}{m}\Bigr)\frac{m}{n}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right),
\qquad
\frac{m}{a_{n}}=O\!\bigl(n^{-1-\varepsilon/m}\bigr),
\]
and (4) yields  

\[
E_{n}=m+\varepsilon+O\!\bigl(n^{-\varepsilon/m}\bigr)\longrightarrow m+\varepsilon .
\]

Because $\varepsilon>0$ is arbitrary, the constant $m$ in (1) cannot be increased. \blacksquare 



  
Part 3.  Asymptotic description of the extremals  
  

We prove that (2) is equivalent to the pair of conditions (3).

Necessity.  
Assume $E_{n}\to m$.  From (4) we have  

\[
E_{n}-m=\frac{m}{b_{n}}+(n+m)\Bigl(\frac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0. \tag{8}
\]

Step 1.  Show that $b_{n}\to\infty$ (i.e. (3a)).  
Suppose, to the contrary, that $b_{n}$ is not unbounded.  
Then there exist $B>0$ and an infinite subsequence $(n_{k})$ with $b_{n_{k}}\le B$.  
Let  

\[
\varepsilon:=\frac{m}{2B}>0 .
\]

Because $E_{n}\to m$, we can choose this subsequence so that  

\[
\lvert E_{n_{k}}-m\rvert<\varepsilon \qquad\text{for all }k. \tag{9}
\]

Applying (8) to these indices and using $b_{n_{k}}\le B$ gives  

\[
(n_{k}+m)\Bigl(1-\tfrac{b_{n_{k}+m}}{b_{n_{k}}}\Bigr)
      =\frac{m}{b_{n_{k}}}-(E_{n_{k}}-m)
      \ge\frac{m}{B}-\varepsilon
      =\varepsilon . \tag{10}
\]

Hence  

\[
b_{n_{k}+m}\;\le\;b_{n_{k}}\Bigl(1-\frac{\varepsilon}{n_{k}+m}\Bigr). \tag{11}
\]

Iterating (11) over the arithmetic progression $n_{k,r}:=n_{k}+rm$ $(r\ge 0)$ gives  

\[
b_{n_{k,r}}\;\le\;B
\exp\!\Bigl(-\varepsilon\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}\frac{1}{n_{k}+jm+m}\Bigr)
\longrightarrow 0\quad(r\to\infty). \tag{12}
\]

Choose $r$ so large that  

\[
b_{n_{k,r}}\;<\;\frac{m}{2(n_{k,r}+m)}. \tag{13}
\]

Returning to (4) with $n=n_{k,r}$ and using $\dfrac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}>0$ we obtain  

\[
E_{n_{k,r}}-m
      \;\ge\;\frac{m}{b_{n_{k,r}}}-(n_{k,r}+m)
      \;>\;2(n_{k,r}+m)-(n_{k,r}+m)
      \;=\;n_{k,r}+m .
\]

Thus $E_{n_{k,r}}-m$ is arbitrarily large, contradicting $E_{n}\to m$.  
Therefore the assumption was false and $b_{n}\to\infty$.  This proves (3a).

Step 2.  Deduce (3b).  
Since $b_{n}\to\infty$, the first term on the right-hand side of (8) tends to $0$; hence  

\[
(n+m)\Bigl(\tfrac{b_{n+m}}{b_{n}}-1\Bigr)\longrightarrow 0 ,
\]
which is exactly (3b).  Necessity is established.

Sufficiency.  
Conversely, if (3a)-(3b) hold, then $\dfrac{m}{b_{n}}\to 0$ and the last term in (4) also tends to $0$, so $E_{n}\to m$. \blacksquare 



  
Examples of extremal sequences  
  

1. $a_{n}=n(\log n)^{\alpha}$, $\alpha>0$.  
   Then $L_{n}=(\log n)^{\alpha}\to\infty$, and a direct calculation gives  
\[
(n+m)\bigl(L_{n+m}/L_{n}-1\bigr)
      =\frac{\alpha m}{\log n}+O\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{(\log n)^{2}}\bigr)\longrightarrow 0.
\]

2. $a_{n}=n\exp\!\bigl[(\log\log n)^{\beta}\bigr]$, $0<\beta<1$.  
   Here $\log L_{n}=(\log\log n)^{\beta}$, and  
\[
\log\!\frac{L_{n+m}}{L_{n}}
      =(\log\log(n+m))^{\beta}-(\log\log n)^{\beta}
      =O\!\Bigl(\frac{1}{n(\log n)^{1-\beta}}\Bigr),
\]
so $(3)$ is satisfied.

3. More generally, any $a_{n}=nL_{n}$ with $L_{n}$ slowly varying (with step $m$) and $L_{n}\to\infty$ fulfils $E_{n}\to m$, and every sequence with this property is of that form. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-order interaction:   
  The expression now links the term aₙ to a_{n+m} across an arbitrary positive
  offset m.  The proof must control the evolution of the sequence in jumps of
  length m, rather than in consecutive terms.

• Divergent series with stride m:  
  Deriving the contradiction requires building an m-step harmonic series
  ∑_{j}1/(k+jm), not the ordinary harmonic series that sufficed in the
  original problem.  Handling this necessitates a careful index-shifting
  argument and a non-trivial comparison with the usual ∑1/ℓ.

• Parameter dependence:  
  Both the inequality and the optimal constant depend explicitly on m.  One
  must show simultaneously that  
  (a) the lower bound scales exactly with m, and  
  (b) the bound is tight for every m, by constructing appropriate families of
  test sequences.  This introduces an additional layer of quantification and
  verification absent from the original kernel.

• More intricate recursion:  
  Instead of a single forward estimate, the proof demands an m-step recursive
  inequality and its iteration, leading to a telescoping sum with variable
  denominators.  Careful bookkeeping of these denominators is essential to
  guarantee divergence.

Overall, the enhanced variant forces competitors to generalise the harmonic-series
argument, manage non-unit step sizes, and track a parameter that permeates every
stage of the reasoning, making the problem substantially more technical and
conceptually demanding than the original.
